label: SES-070 PI-030 architecture planning — close-out payload extensions and apply integration. Three stop-the-flow decisions settled (Q0 full scope, Q1 emit-time helper, Q2 conversation block) plus one decide-and-announce captured as decision (resolves status-flip via extended POST /references). Three slice prompts authored (A: methodology + resolves; B: apply extensions; C: enumerate_commits.py helper).

session.title: PI-030 architecture planning — full-scope close-out payload extension + apply_close_out.py integration: scope, helper architecture, conversation gap, resolves status-flip. Three Claude Code slice prompts authored (A, B, C) plus close-out payload and matching apply prompt. Methodology §4 amendment scheduled for slice A. No new planning items.
session.conversation_reference: Claude.ai ARCHITECTURE-mode planning conversation opened against the PI-030 kickoff at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-030-A-apply-close-out-commits-section-kickoff.md (committed at cc05211). Conversation followed the kickoff's surface-and-settle structure but diverged on scope: the kickoff narrowed PI-030 to commits only while methodology §8 names PI-030 as covering all four new payload sections (commits, work_tickets, resolves_planning_items, addresses_planning_items). The scope question (Q0) was elevated as the first stop-the-flow and settled in favor of methodology §8's full scope, which also surfaced a fifth section (conversation block) that the methodology assumed but didn't specify (Q2). Q1 (helper architecture) was settled in favor of emit-time discovery with full records in the payload per methodology §4.1's example. A fourth consequential decision (DEC-224) captured the implementation choice to extend POST /references for atomic edge+flip rather than add a dedicated endpoint. Slicing settled as three Claude Code prompts (A: methodology + resolves; B: apply extensions; C: helper) with explicit dependency ordering (A before B; C independent). Conversation made minimal commits during the planning turns (research-only views and grep calls); commits in this session are the four authoring files plus this close-out — all pushed at session close per sandbox convention.
session.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Project default operating mode: ARCHITECTURE. ... PI-030 — apply_close_out.py Integration for the Commits Close-Out Payload Section — Kickoff Prompt" (the full kickoff document was provided as an attached document; the conversation opened against it after Claude confirmed crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md as the orientation doc).

The conversation covered: (1) Pre-work read of crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md plus the six required items on the kickoff's read list (commit.md, methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md, apply_close_out.py, commits access layer + REST router, ses_067.json as payload-shape reference) — confirmed slice B baseline (1481 passed + 3 skipped, SHA-uniqueness pre-check in access layer maps to HTTP 409 with existing identifier, conversation/session/decision/PI heads in the snapshot); (2) Q0 (PI-030 scope) elevated as stop-the-flow: kickoff narrowed to commits only but methodology §8 names PI-030 as covering all four new payload sections — settled to A1 full scope (all four methodology-named sections); (3) Q1 (commit-metadata helper architecture) settled to Option A — emit-time discovery, full records in payload per methodology §4.1's example, helper at crmbuilder-v2/scripts/enumerate_commits.py invoked from sandbox at close-out authoring time, decoupling from apply-script git dependency; (4) Q2 (conversation gap) elevated as stop-the-flow after verifying 45 conversation records exist for 68 sessions with no conversation records for SES-061 onward (the planning-conversation cohort PI-030 belongs to) — settled to H1: close-out payload gains a conversation block, methodology §4 amended to add §4.0 specifying the new section's shape and authoring rules; (5) decide-and-announce: resolves status-flip implemented as extension of POST /references rather than dedicated endpoint, captured as DEC-224 for explicit governance; (6) slicing settled as three Claude Code prompts (A methodology + resolves server-side; B apply_close_out.py extensions for five new sections including vocab additions for wrote_record target types; C standalone enumerate_commits.py helper) with explicit predecessor/successor ordering — A before B, C independent; (7) close-out artifacts (this payload + apply prompt + three slice prompts) authored and committed to crmbuilder repo in sandbox before close. Identifier-head verification: SES-068 is the current applied head; SES-069 is queued (PI-023 reconciliation utility planning, not yet applied); this conversation claims SES-070, DEC-221..224. No new planning items — PI-030 stays Open until the slice prompts execute and complete the build work.
session.artifacts_produced:
Five Claude.ai-sandbox-produced deliverables, all committed at this conversation's close:

1. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_070.json — this close-out payload. Four decisions (DEC-221 PI-030 scope is full four-section payload extension per methodology §8; DEC-222 commit-metadata helper runs emit-time with full records in payload per methodology §4.1 example; DEC-223 close-out payload gains conversation block, methodology §4 amended to add §4.0; DEC-224 POST /references extended for atomic edge+flip on resolves kind). No new planning items. Five references (four decided_in edges from each new DEC to SES-070, one is_about edge from SES-070 to PI-030).

2. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-070.md — apply prompt that POSTs this payload to the local V2 API at http://127.0.0.1:8765, captures identifier heads before and after, verifies snapshot regeneration and change_log audit row, commits the snapshot delta in a single commit per the no-push Claude Code convention.

3. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-030-A-resolves-flip-and-methodology.md — slice A Claude Code prompt. Two coupled changes: (a) references.create() extended so that when relationship_kind == 'resolves', the same transaction also calls planning_items.update(target_id, status='Resolved'), idempotent on already-Resolved targets; (b) methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md amended to add §4.0 conversation block paragraph with JSON example and update §4's apply-ordering bullet to insert 'conversation' between 'session' and 'work_tickets'. 5 new tests on top of slice B baseline.

4. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-030-B-apply-close-out-extensions.md — slice B Claude Code prompt. Extends apply_close_out.py to honor methodology §4 apply ordering for all nine sections (session → conversation → work_tickets → planning_items → commits → decisions → references → resolves_planning_items → addresses_planning_items) with per-section shape functions handling the four transforms (work_ticket addresses_planning_item → embedded reference, commit commit_conversation_id ← conversation block, resolves/addresses planning_items → POST /references with appropriate kind). Also extends vocab.py to admit conversation/work_ticket/commit as deposit_event_wrote_record target_types so the access-layer invariant sum(records_summary) == len(wrote_records) holds for the new types. ~15 new tests on top of slice A baseline.

5. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-030-C-enumerate-commits-helper.md — slice C Claude Code prompt. Authors crmbuilder-v2/scripts/enumerate_commits.py, a standalone CLI that reads db-export/commits.json for per-repo last-ingested SHA, runs git pull --ff-only, enumerates commits via git log with ASCII control-character separators for safe multi-line message parsing, counts files-changed via git diff-tree, emits JSON array suitable to paste into a close-out payload's commits section. CLI: --repos, --engagement-db-export, --repo-root, --repo-root-override, --branch, --skip-pull, --output. 8 new tests.
session.in_flight_at_end:
PI-030's slice prompts queue for Claude Code execution. Dependency order: slice A must apply before slice B (B's apply extensions call A's extended resolves endpoint); slice C is independent (sandbox-side helper, no API or apply-script dependency). After all three slices land, the next close-out payload (whichever conversation authors one) can use the full nine-section format: session → conversation → work_tickets → planning_items → commits → decisions → references → resolves_planning_items → addresses_planning_items.

Known follow-on work surfaced by this conversation but not authored here: (i) PI-024/PI-025 backfill of conversation records for SES-001..SES-046 and SES-056..SES-069 — the conversation gap that Q2 closed for going-forward sessions remains for historical ones; (ii) PI-031 Commits panel UI under the Governance sidebar — outside PI-030 scope per the kickoff; (iii) PI-033 historical commit back-fill — outside PI-030 scope, downstream of PI-030's machinery.

PI-030 status: stays Open at this conversation's close. The conversation produces architecture decisions and slice prompts, not the build itself; PI-030 resolves when the three slice prompts execute and pass their acceptance tests. PI-027 (methodology document) and PI-028 (commit schema spec) remain Open per methodology §9 — PI-033 retroactively resolves both via the resolves_planning_items section that PI-030's slice B brings online.

For the next planning conversation: Doug's next move after the apply prompt for this close-out is to run the three slice prompts in order (A, B, C) at his terminal via Claude Code. After all three land, the next sandbox conversation can produce a close-out using the full nine-section format and the enumerate_commits.py helper to populate the commits array. The first conversation to use the new format will likely be either a PI-031 (Commits panel UI) planning conversation or a fresh CBM-domain conversation depending on Doug's priorities.

decisions.title: PI-030 scope is the full four-section close-out payload extension per methodology §8, not commits-only — kickoff's narrowing was editorial drift
decisions.context: The PI-030 kickoff prompt narrowed scope to 'the commits close-out payload section' and named only commits in its goal statement, scope language, and out-of-scope list. Methodology §8, however, assigns PI-030 the full scope: 'Close-out payload schema extensions for the four new sections; apply_close_out.py updates; enumerate_commits.py helper.' The four sections per methodology §4 are commits, work_tickets, resolves_planning_items, and addresses_planning_items. The kickoff's narrowing is silent on whether the other three sections defer to follow-on planning items (PI-049, PI-050, PI-051) or are folded back into PI-030 implicitly. The audit chain methodology §6 names depends on resolves edges landing transactionally with status flips and addresses edges populating governance — neither of which works without those sections shipping. The decision is whether to honor the kickoff's narrowing (defer three sections) or restore the methodology's stated full scope.
decisions.decision: PI-030 ships all four methodology-named close-out payload sections (commits, work_tickets, resolves_planning_items, addresses_planning_items) plus the conversation block surfaced by DEC-223 — five new sections total. The build slicing is three Claude Code prompts: slice A (methodology amendment + resolves status-flip server behavior), slice B (apply_close_out.py extensions for all five new sections), slice C (enumerate_commits.py helper). Slice A precedes slice B; slice C is independent.
decisions.rationale: Methodology §8 already committed to this scope, and PI-030's kickoff cites methodology as its spec authority. The narrowing reads as editorial drift rather than a deliberate slicing decision — the kickoff doesn't articulate why three sections would defer or what the follow-on plan would be. Settling the full set in one architecture conversation lets us answer apply-ordering once (methodology §4 specifies session → work_tickets → planning_items → commits → decisions → references → resolves_planning_items → addresses_planning_items — and amended by DEC-223 to insert conversation between session and work_tickets), whereas three smaller architecture conversations would answer the same question three times. The four sections are tightly coupled: work_tickets reference planning_items, commits depend on conversations that record sessions, resolves and addresses edges flow from conversations to planning_items. Splitting them creates the same architecture surface in pieces with no real benefit.
decisions.alternatives_considered: A2: narrow scope — ship only the commits section in PI-030; defer work_tickets, resolves_planning_items, addresses_planning_items to PI-049, PI-050, PI-051. Rejected because (i) methodology already named all four for PI-030, (ii) the audit chain the methodology motivates depends on all four sections shipping, (iii) three separate architecture conversations to answer the same coupled questions wastes effort, (iv) the kickoff's narrowing didn't articulate a reason. A3: even broader — fold PI-031 (Commits UI) and PI-033 (back-fill) into PI-030. Rejected because the UI and back-fill are genuinely separable downstream concerns and the existing planning item structure (PI-031, PI-033) already captures them.
decisions.consequences: PI-030's three slice prompts (A, B, C) carry more total content than the kickoff anticipated — roughly 2x the test count and a fifth section in the payload. The methodology document gets a §4.0 amendment (per DEC-223). The vocab.py change to admit conversation/work_ticket/commit as wrote_record target_types is part of slice B. The first close-out authored after all three slices land can ingest the full machinery including historical-data-aware commit ranges. The PI-024/PI-025 backfill of historical conversation records for SES-001..SES-069 remains a separate concern not addressed by PI-030.

decisions.title: Commit-metadata helper runs emit-time, sandbox-side, with full records in the payload — apply stays a dumb POSTer with no git dependency
decisions.context: The PI-030 kickoff framed three options for where the helper that turns 'this conversation's commits' into governance records actually runs: (A) emit-time in the sandbox, payload carries full records; (B) apply-time in Doug's local clone, payload carries a SHA-only manifest, apply does git log lookup; (C) hybrid where the sandbox emits SHAs plus the producing conversation attribution and the apply backfills metadata. Methodology §4.1's example payload already shows full commit records, not a SHA-only manifest, partially constraining the choice. The trade-off: emit-time discovery (A) keeps the payload self-describing and the apply simple but requires the sandbox to have a clone of every repo the conversation touched; apply-time discovery (B/C) gets canonical git metadata at the cost of subprocess-to-git dependency in the apply script and a less self-describing payload.
decisions.decision: The helper runs emit-time, sandbox-side, at a standalone script crmbuilder-v2/scripts/enumerate_commits.py invoked at close-out authoring time. The script reads db-export/commits.json for per-repo last-ingested SHA, runs git pull --ff-only per repo, enumerates commits via git log with ASCII control-character separators (US/RS) for safe multi-line message parsing, counts files-changed via git diff-tree, and emits a JSON array suitable to paste into the payload's commits section. The payload carries full commit records (SHA + message_first_line + message_full + author_name + author_email + committed_at + repository + branch + parent_shas + files_changed_count) per methodology §4.1's example. apply_close_out.py POSTs each record as-is and adds no git subprocess dependency.
decisions.rationale: Three reasons in priority order: (i) methodology §4.1 already specifies the full-record payload shape and this conversation has no mandate to deviate from it; (ii) the apply stays simple and matches the existing pattern (decisions and references are authored at emit time, not looked up at apply time) — the 'separation of concerns' argument the kickoff floated for option C is misapplied because decisions are also 'expensive content the emitter authors' and we don't split decisions; (iii) the payload is self-describing for audit, which matters because anyone reading the payload sees the commit content rather than just an opaque SHA list. The sandbox-visibility concern (the sandbox can't see commits Doug made locally but never pushed) applies equally to options B and C — an unpushed commit is invisible to the API anyway, so the helper missing it at emit time doesn't lose data that would have been findable later.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B (apply-time discovery, SHA-only manifest): rejected because it introduces a subprocess-to-git dependency in apply_close_out.py with no compensating benefit. The apply script becomes harder to test in isolation, and the payload becomes less audit-friendly. Option C (hybrid SHA-plus-attribution in payload, apply backfills metadata): rejected as functionally equivalent to B for the same reasons — the only meaningful difference from B is one extra field per payload entry (commit_repository), which doesn't justify the architectural cost. A variant of A where the helper is folded into apply_close_out.py with auto-detection of payload format: rejected because folding tools into the apply script bloats it and obscures the separation-of-concerns (the helper is sandbox-side; the apply is local-side).
decisions.consequences: The sandbox must have a valid git clone of every repo the conversation produces commits in. For most PI-030-style conversations that's just crmbuilder; cross-repo conversations (methodology rollout, multi-domain CBM work) require the sandbox to clone the other repo too. The helper accepts a --repo-root-override REPO=PATH flag to handle this. Risk: the sandbox sees stale state if Doug pushes a manual commit between sandbox init and close-out emit — mitigated by the helper's mandatory git pull --ff-only before enumeration. Edge case: manual commits Doug never pushes are invisible to either approach. The convention captured in slice C and surfaced to repo CLAUDE.md (during slice A): 'push any manual commits before opening a planning conversation that will close out.'

decisions.title: Close-out payload format gains a conversation block; methodology §4 amended to add §4.0 — closes the FK gap between commits and the conversation entity
decisions.context: Commits' commit_conversation_id is a hard foreign-key enforced by the access layer's _require_conversation_exists check (a session.get(Conversation, conversation_id) lookup; missing CONV → HTTP 422). The current db-export snapshot has 45 conversation records for 68 sessions, with no conversation records for SES-061 onward — the recent planning-conversation cohort PI-030 belongs to. Methodology §4.1 says commits' conversation_id is 'the close-out's owning conversation; no explicit field needed in the payload entry' but never specifies the mechanism for finding or creating that conversation. Recent close-out payloads (ses_067, ses_068, ses_069) carry no conversation block and no conversation_records_session edge. Without resolution, PI-030's commit ingestion would fail at apply time with conversation_id_not_found.
decisions.decision: The close-out payload format gains a top-level conversation block parallel to the existing session block. The conversation POST is one apply step (between session and work_tickets), and it carries embedded references including the conversation_records_session edge to the session in the same atomic POST. Methodology §4 is amended to add a new §4.0 'conversation block' paragraph with JSON example, update the apply-ordering bullet at the top of §4 to insert 'conversation' between 'session' and 'work_tickets', and add a row to the change log dated 05-24-26 documenting the amendment. The conversation block is required for any close-out that includes a commits section; optional when commits are absent. The conversation_identifier is computed client-side via GET /conversations/next-identifier. Backfill of conversation records for SES-001..SES-069 remains PI-024/PI-025 scope.
decisions.rationale: Three reasons: (i) the payload-as-spec discipline already established for every other governance entity — decisions are explicit in the payload, references are explicit, planning items are explicit; conversations should be too. The alternative of apply-internal magic (lazy-creating a conversation when commits are seen) introduces implicit machinery the payload doesn't describe, which is harder to audit. (ii) The H1 path doesn't expand PI-030 scope into the session endpoint (H3's drawback) — modifying /sessions to auto-create a paired conversation would touch a stable endpoint with no commits relationship, dragging unrelated concerns into PI-030. (iii) The methodology amendment is small (one new paragraph + one updated line) and the cost per close-out is modest (author writes five extra fields at close-out time).
decisions.alternatives_considered: H2: apply lazy-creates a conversation when commits are present. Rejected because (i) it adds apply-internal magic the payload doesn't describe, breaking the payload-as-spec invariant; (ii) the conversation's title/purpose would have to be synthesized from session fields, producing low-quality metadata that the explicit-author path avoids; (iii) it doesn't help backfill (PI-024/PI-025 still needs to add historical conversations). H3: modify the /sessions POST endpoint to atomically create a paired CONV record + conversation_records_session edge. Rejected because (i) it touches an endpoint with no commits relationship, expanding PI-030 scope; (ii) it doesn't backfill old sessions; (iii) the same machinery has to be added later via PI-024/PI-025 anyway. Defer-and-pre-create: have Doug manually create CONV records before opening planning conversations. Rejected because it puts operational friction at the wrong layer — the close-out is the natural authoring moment for the conversation record, not a separate manual step.
decisions.consequences: Methodology document gets one new sub-section §4.0 plus the apply-ordering update and the change-log row — slice A applies this. Every close-out from PI-030 forward includes a conversation block when commits are present. The apply script's section ordering becomes nine entries (session → conversation → work_tickets → planning_items → commits → decisions → references → resolves_planning_items → addresses_planning_items). The conversation_identifier propagates through the apply run to populate every commit's commit_conversation_id (slice B handles this via a per-section shape function). Historical conversations for SES-001..SES-069 remain absent until PI-024/PI-025 backfill runs; conversation records produced by this PI-030 conversation and later are reliably present.

decisions.title: POST /references extended to atomically flip target planning_item status on resolves kind — single endpoint handles both write paths, no dedicated /resolves endpoint
decisions.context: Methodology §4.3 specifies that resolves_planning_items entries 'generate one conversation → resolves → planning_item edge AND flip the planning item's status from Open to Resolved in the same atomic transaction.' The apply script can't make the operation atomic from outside (two separate POSTs leave a window where the edge exists but the status hasn't flipped); the transaction must be server-side. Two implementation paths: (i) extend the existing POST /references endpoint so that when relationship_kind == 'resolves' and source_type == 'conversation' and target_type == 'planning_item', the access layer also performs planning_items.update(target_id, status='Resolved') in the same DB transaction; (ii) add a dedicated endpoint, e.g., POST /conversations/{conv_id}/resolves/{pi_id}, that does both operations explicitly. The choice affects API surface, discoverability, and the apply script's logic.
decisions.decision: POST /references is extended to handle the atomic edge+flip for resolves kind. references.create() detects relationship == 'resolves' after creating the reference row and, in the same transaction, calls planning_items.update(target_id, status='Resolved'). Idempotent on already-Resolved targets (no-op update; reference still created). Source/target validation is delegated to the existing _kinds_for_pair (vocab.py admits resolves only for (conversation, planning_item) pairs, rejecting any other shape with HTTP 422 before this code runs). No new endpoint is added.
decisions.rationale: The apply script's logic stays simple: POST /references with the appropriate relationship; the server decides whether the edge alone or edge-plus-flip applies. Adding a dedicated endpoint would have required the apply to know two different endpoints (one for ordinary refs, one for resolves) and to dispatch correctly per section, which is more apply-side complexity for the same outcome. The 'magic' is localized to the resolves kind, which is new to vocab.py and already special — the same vocab block that enables resolves also gates source/target compatibility. The deposit_event POST already follows the same pattern (POST /deposit-events does row + parent edge + wrote_record back-refs + state transition in one transaction); the resolves extension matches that precedent rather than inventing a new one.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Dedicated endpoint POST /conversations/{conv_id}/resolves/{pi_id}: rejected because (i) the apply script gains a new endpoint to remember without a clear discoverability benefit, (ii) the dedicated endpoint duplicates the same edge-creation logic for one kind, (iii) the dedicated endpoint name conveys 'conversation does the resolving' framing but the same is true of the references endpoint with relationship=resolves. Magic-extension of POST /planning-items/{pi_id} to accept a 'resolved_by_conversation' field that triggers the flip: rejected because it shifts the operation to the wrong entity (the conversation is the actor; the planning item is the object — putting the action on the planning_item endpoint inverts that). Keep the operation as two separate non-atomic POSTs (apply does POST /references then PATCH /planning-items/{pi}): rejected because it violates the methodology's atomic-transaction requirement and leaves a race window.
decisions.consequences: Slice A implements the extension as a four-line addition to references.create() (an if-branch invoking planning_items.update after the existing edge insert + flush). The behavior is gated on relationship == 'resolves', so all other reference kinds are unaffected. Tests cover the flip happy path (Open → Resolved), idempotency on already-Resolved targets, duplicate-edge 409 behavior, and the non-resolves no-op. Future extensions following the same pattern (e.g., a hypothetical 'closes' kind that flips a different status) inherit the precedent: extend POST /references for the new magic kind rather than adding dedicated endpoints. The decision is reviewable if more such kinds emerge — at some threshold (probably 3+ magic kinds), a refactor to a generic 'side-effect dispatcher' may be warranted.
